title: How Do I Stop Worrying About Things I Can't Control?
keyword: how do I stop worrying about things I can't control
excerpt: You rarely stop worrying about what you can't control by trying harder — you stop by sorting what's actually yours to act on.
content:
If you find yourself worrying about things you have no real influence over — another person's choices, an outcome that is already decided, the future in general — telling yourself to "just stop" rarely works. **What usually helps is sorting what is genuinely yours to act on from what is not, and redirecting your energy accordingly.**

## Productive concern vs. worry

Productive concern leads somewhere: it identifies a risk and points to an action you can take about it. Worry, by contrast, circles the same territory without ever producing a next step, because there is no next step available — the outcome depends on someone else, or has already been set in motion. The two can feel identical in the moment, which is part of why worry is so hard to walk away from: it feels like you are doing something useful, even when you are not. Many people describe worry as a kind of insurance — as if enough anticipation could soften a bad outcome or prevent it altogether — even though, rationally, they know that isn't how it works.

This is one of the more counterintuitive things about worry: it often persists specifically because it feels responsible. Letting go of a worry about something you cannot control can feel, at a gut level, like carelessness — even when logically you know continuing to worry changes nothing about the outcome.

## Identifying what is actually yours to control

A useful first step is to ask, plainly, of any worry: is there an action I can take today that would change this? If the answer is yes, that is productive concern, and the next move is to take the action, even a small one. If the answer is no — if the outcome depends entirely on someone else's decision, or on something that has already happened — that worry belongs in a different category, and continuing to circle it will not change the result. This distinction sounds simple, but in practice it takes deliberate effort, because the mind often blurs the line between "caring about this" and "doing something about this," treating them as the same activity when they are not.

It can help to be specific rather than general when applying this test. "I'm worried about my daughter's future" is too broad to act on. "I'm worried she won't get into the program she wants" can be broken down further: is there a concrete action available (helping with an application, offering encouragement) versus a decision that is entirely outside your hands (the admissions committee's choice)?

## A simple sorting exercise

Writing the worry down and splitting it into two columns can make this distinction concrete: what depends on me, and what does not. Items in the first column get a specific next action. Items in the second column get acknowledged — named out loud or on paper — and then deliberately set aside, not because the concern does not matter, but because continuing to hold it does not change the outcome.

This is uncomfortable at first, especially if you are used to believing that worrying is a form of care or vigilance. But there is a difference between caring about an outcome and believing that worrying about it is doing something on your behalf. Practicing this sorting exercise repeatedly, even when it feels artificial at first, tends to make the distinction more automatic over time. Some people find it helpful to physically cross out or highlight items in the second column, as a visual signal that the item has been acknowledged and does not need to be revisited today.

## Why this feels harder under stress

This sorting process tends to be noticeably harder during periods of general overwhelm, when your overall capacity for calm reasoning is already reduced. On a demanding week, even a worry you would normally sort easily into "outside my control" can feel harder to release. Recognizing that the difficulty is partly about your current capacity, not a sign the technique has stopped working, can prevent the added frustration of feeling like you've failed at something you previously found manageable.

## Redirecting energy to next steps

Once a worry is clearly outside your control, the more useful question becomes: what can I do for myself while this plays out? That might mean preparing for a few likely outcomes rather than one imagined worst case, or simply returning attention to something within your control right now — a task, a conversation, a decision that is actually yours. Over time, this redirection is what reduces how much space uncontrollable worries take up, more than any single relaxation technique.

## Why this is harder with people you care about

This pattern becomes especially difficult when the uncontrollable thing involves someone you love — their choices, their health, their happiness. It can feel almost wrong to "let go" of worry about someone you care about, as if doing so meant caring less. In practice, redirecting your energy toward what you can actually offer — support, availability, honest conversation — tends to serve the relationship better than circling a worry that changes nothing about their situation. This does not mean the worry disappears entirely; it means it takes up less continuous space in your day, freeing up capacity to actually be present with the person you are worried about, rather than distracted by rehearsing scenarios about them.

## What to do when the same worry keeps returning

Some worries do not stay sorted after one pass — they resurface days or weeks later, especially if the underlying situation remains unresolved. This is normal, and it does not mean the sorting exercise failed. Each time the worry returns, briefly repeating the same process — is there something new I can act on, or is this still outside my control — tends to be more effective than assuming you need a completely different approach each time.

## Building tolerance for uncertainty over time

Much of the discomfort in this pattern comes not from the worry itself but from a broader difficulty tolerating uncertainty. Each time you practice sorting a worry and consciously setting aside the part that isn't yours to control, you are also practicing tolerating not knowing how something will turn out. This tolerance tends to build gradually, the same way any skill does — through repeated, deliberate practice rather than through a single decision to be more comfortable with uncertainty. Over months, many people notice that situations which once triggered significant worry now register as mild background noise instead.

If this pattern shows up frequently across different parts of your life, it may be connected to a broader overthinking pattern — the [complete guide to overthinking and racing thoughts](#pillar) covers this in more depth, including what tends to keep the pattern going.

Learning to tell the difference between productive concern and worry that has nowhere to go is a skill that takes repetition, not a single insight. If this feels like part of a larger pattern that is hard to interrupt on your own, [scheduling an initial consultation](#consultation) can help clarify what is underneath it. If you would rather explore it at your own pace first, [the private community](#community) is a good place to start.

This article is educational and reflects a non-clinical coaching perspective. Sal Ray is not a therapist, psychologist, or medical provider, and this content does not diagnose or treat any condition.

## Frequently Asked Questions

faq.question: Why can't I just decide to stop worrying?
faq.answer: Because worry often feels like a form of vigilance or care, deciding to stop can feel like you're abandoning the outcome. It's usually more effective to sort the worry into what you can act on versus what you can't, rather than trying to suppress it outright.

faq.question: Is it wrong to worry about things I can't control?
faq.answer: It's a very common pattern, not a character flaw. The goal isn't to never feel concern — it's to notice when concern has nowhere productive to go, and redirect that energy toward what is actually within your influence.

faq.question: What if the worry is about something serious, like a loved one's health?
faq.answer: Serious worries are harder to set aside, and that's understandable. The same sorting still applies: identify anything you can genuinely do (asking questions, offering support, preparing practically) and separate that from circling the same fear without a next step.

faq.question: How long does it take to reduce this kind of worry?
faq.answer: It varies, but most people notice a shift within a few weeks of consistently practicing the sorting exercise, especially when paired with redirecting attention to something concrete and within their control.
